title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make rich, authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with our easy-to-follow recipe. Perfect for a taste of traditional Mexican cuisine!
seo.h1: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe: Pollo en Mole
h2Headings: What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?
Ingredients You Need for Chicken Mole
Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Results
Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole
Variations and Substitutions for Mole Sauce
seo.imageAlt: A plate of authentic chicken mole (pollo en mole) served with rice and tortillas, garnished with sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of homemade Chicken Mole, a classic Mexican dish.

description: Dive into the soulful depths of traditional Mexican cuisine with this rich, complex, and utterly satisfying Chicken Mole Recipe. This Pollo en Mole features tender chicken bathed in a velvety, multi-layered sauce, boasting a harmonious blend of smoky chiles, aromatic spices, nuts, and a hint of chocolate. It's a beloved dish, cherished for its profound flavors and comforting warmth, perfect for a special family meal.

ingredients.item: Bone-in, skin-on chicken pieces (thighs, drumsticks, or a mix)

ingredients.notes: for cooking chicken, reserve broth for mole
stemmed, seeded, and deveined (optional, for extra smoke/heat)

ingredients.item: Stale tortilla or bolillo bread slice

instructions.title: Cook the Chicken and Prepare Broth
instructions.text: Place chicken pieces in a large pot with 6-8 cups of water or broth, 1/2 onion, 2 garlic cloves, bay leaves, and 1 tsp salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until chicken is tender and cooked through. Remove chicken, reserving 4-5 cups of the cooking broth. Set chicken aside to cool slightly, then shred or debone if desired.

instructions.title: Prepare and Toast Chiles
instructions.text: Lightly toast the dried Ancho, Mulato, Pasilla, and Chipotle Morita chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for 30-60 seconds per side, being careful not to burn them (this releases their aroma). Immediately transfer toasted chiles to a bowl and cover with hot reserved chicken broth or water. Let them soak for 20-30 minutes until softened. Drain, reserving the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Roast Aromatics and Toast Spices/Nuts
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, roast the halved Roma tomatoes, tomatillos, 1/4 onion, and 3 garlic cloves until softened and slightly charred, about 5-7 minutes. Set aside. In a separate dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, and sesame seeds over medium-low heat until fragrant and lightly golden, about 3-5 minutes. Add raisins, cinnamon stick, anise seeds, cloves, peppercorns, and cumin seeds, and toast for another minute. Remove from heat.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: In a blender, combine the softened chiles, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, garlic, toasted nuts, seeds, raisins, spices, and the stale tortilla/bread. Add 1-2 cups of the reserved chile soaking liquid or chicken broth. Blend until a very smooth paste forms. This may take several minutes and require stopping to scrape down the sides. For an extra smooth sauce, strain the paste through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing firmly to extract all liquid, discarding solids. This is a crucial step for a refined sauce.

instructions.title: Simmer the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat 2 tbsp of lard or vegetable oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot oil. It will splatter, so be cautious. Cook, stirring constantly, for 10-15 minutes until the sauce darkens, thickens, and loses its raw chile taste. This 'frying' step is essential for developing the mole's deep flavor. Gradually stir in 3-4 cups of the reserved chicken broth, a little at a time, until the sauce reaches your desired consistency. Add the Mexican chocolate and stir until completely melted and incorporated. Season with salt to taste, and add a teaspoon of sugar or piloncillo if needed to balance the flavors.

instructions.title: Combine Chicken and Serve
instructions.text: Add the cooked chicken pieces (whole or shredded) to the simmering mole sauce. Stir gently to coat the chicken completely. Continue to simmer for another 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the chicken to heat through. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust seasonings one last time. Serve your authentic Pollo en Mole hot.

tips: **Don't Burn the Chiles:** When toasting dried chiles, watch them carefully. They should become fragrant, not smoky or black. Burnt chiles will make your mole bitter. A quick toast is all you need.

**Achieve a Smooth Sauce:** After blending, straining the mole through a fine-mesh sieve is highly recommended for a truly silky, restaurant-quality texture. It removes any remaining chile skins or seed fragments.

**Balance the Flavors:** Mole is all about balance. Taste frequently during the simmering stage. If it's too spicy, a bit more chocolate or a pinch of sugar can mellow it. If it lacks depth, a little more salt or a prolonged simmer can help.
article:
## What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?

Chicken Mole, known in Spanish as Pollo en Mole, is one of the most iconic and revered dishes in Mexican cuisine. Far from a simple sauce, mole is a culinary masterpiece, often comprising dozens of ingredients meticulously prepared to create a deeply complex and harmonious flavor profile. While there are many types of mole across Mexico, such as Mole Poblano from Puebla or Mole Negro from Oaxaca, they all share a common thread of rich, multi-layered tastes, often combining smoky chiles, aromatic spices, nuts, seeds, and a hint of chocolate. It's a dish traditionally reserved for celebrations and special occasions, embodying the heart and soul of Mexican culinary heritage. This **Chicken Mole Recipe** offers an authentic journey into its profound flavors, promising a truly unforgettable dining experience.

## Ingredients You Need for Chicken Mole

Crafting an authentic mole requires a thoughtful selection of ingredients, each playing a vital role in the final symphony of flavors. The foundation of our **authentic Mexican chicken mole** lies in a blend of dried chiles – typically Ancho, Mulato, and Pasilla – which provide smokiness, fruitiness, and mild heat. Beyond the chiles, you'll gather an array of aromatics like tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, and garlic, along with a medley of nuts and seeds (almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds) for body and richness. Spices such as cinnamon, cloves, anise, and cumin contribute to its aromatic complexity, while a touch of traditional Mexican chocolate adds depth, color, and a balancing sweetness without making the sauce overtly sweet. Tender chicken pieces, typically bone-in, are the perfect canvas for this magnificent sauce.

## Step-by-Step Chicken Mole Instructions

Making mole from scratch is a labor of love, but the process, broken down, is incredibly rewarding. First, you'll gently cook your chicken to yield tender meat and a flavorful broth, which is essential for the sauce. Next, the dried chiles are briefly toasted and rehydrated, a crucial step to unlock their full flavor. Then, a collection of aromatics, nuts, and spices are lightly roasted to deepen their character before everything is meticulously blended into a smooth, thick paste. This paste is then slow-cooked in a pot, where it's 'fried' to develop incredible depth, before being thinned with the reserved chicken broth and finished with Mexican chocolate. Follow these detailed steps on **how to make chicken mole** and you'll be well on your way to a culinary triumph.

## Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Results

To achieve the most sublime **Chicken Mole Recipe**, attention to detail is key. Firstly, when toasting your dried chiles, ensure they are merely fragrant and not burnt, as bitterness will ruin the sauce. Secondly, after blending all the ingredients, consider passing the mole paste through a fine-mesh sieve. This extra step removes any fibrous bits, resulting in an incredibly smooth and luxurious sauce texture. Thirdly, the 'frying' of the mole paste in oil is non-negotiable; it helps develop the deep, complex flavors. Finally, don't be afraid to taste and adjust seasonings throughout the simmering process. A pinch of salt, a touch of sugar, or even a bit more chocolate can perfectly balance the mole's intricate profile.

## Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole

Pollo en Mole is a dish that deserves to be the star of your table. Traditionally, it's served with fluffy Mexican red rice, which perfectly soaks up the rich sauce, and warm corn tortillas for scooping every last drop. A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds over the top adds a beautiful garnish and a subtle nutty crunch. For a refreshing contrast, consider serving it with a side of thinly sliced radishes, a simple green salad, or pickled red onions. While this **pollo en mole recipe** is rich and satisfying on its own, these accompaniments elevate the experience, offering textural and flavor counterpoints that make each bite even more delightful. Don't forget a refreshing agua fresca or cold beer to complete the authentic Mexican meal.

## Variations and Substitutions for Mole Sauce

While this **authentic Chicken Mole Recipe** strives for traditional perfection, there are minor variations you can explore. If specific dried chiles are hard to find, a combination of Ancho and Pasilla can still yield a delicious result, though the complexity may vary. For those seeking an **easy chicken mole recipe** without compromising too much on flavor, a high-quality mole paste (such as Dona Maria or Mayordomo) can be used as a base, significantly cutting down on preparation time. Simply fry the paste in oil, thin with chicken broth, and add chocolate before simmering with the chicken. You can also experiment with different nuts, like pecans or cashews, or add a piece of plantain to the blend for extra sweetness and body. Remember, while authenticity is a goal, personal taste and ingredient availability can guide your culinary journey.

jsonLdSchema.name: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce
jsonLdSchema.description: Learn how to make rich, authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with our easy-to-follow recipe. Perfect for a taste of traditional Mexican cuisine!
jsonLdSchema.keywords: Chicken Mole Recipe, how to make chicken mole, easy chicken mole recipe, authentic Mexican chicken mole, pollo en mole recipe, best chicken mole sauce recipe
recipeIngredient: 2.5-3 lbs Bone-in, skin-on chicken pieces (thighs, drumsticks, or a mix)
6-8 cups Chicken broth or water (for cooking chicken, reserve broth for mole)
4 Dried Ancho chiles (stemmed, seeded, and deveined)
3 Dried Mulato chiles (stemmed, seeded, and deveined)
3 Dried Pasilla chiles (stemmed, seeded, and deveined)
2 Dried Chipotle Morita chiles (stemmed, seeded, and deveined)
2 tbsp Sesame seeds (plus extra for garnish)
1 Stale tortilla or bolillo bread slice
1 tsp Sugar or piloncillo (optional, to balance flavor)

recipeInstructions.name: Cook the Chicken and Prepare Broth
recipeInstructions.text: Place chicken pieces in a large pot with 6-8 cups of water or broth, 1/2 onion, 2 garlic cloves, bay leaves, and 1 tsp salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until chicken is tender and cooked through. Remove chicken, reserving 4-5 cups of the cooking broth. Set chicken aside to cool slightly, then shred or debone if desired.

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare and Toast Chiles
recipeInstructions.text: Lightly toast the dried Ancho, Mulato, Pasilla, and Chipotle Morita chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for 30-60 seconds per side, being careful not to burn them (this releases their aroma). Immediately transfer toasted chiles to a bowl and cover with hot reserved chicken broth or water. Let them soak for 20-30 minutes until softened. Drain, reserving the soaking liquid.

recipeInstructions.name: Roast Aromatics and Toast Spices/Nuts
recipeInstructions.text: In the same dry skillet, roast the halved Roma tomatoes, tomatillos, 1/4 onion, and 3 garlic cloves until softened and slightly charred, about 5-7 minutes. Set aside. In a separate dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, and sesame seeds over medium-low heat until fragrant and lightly golden, about 3-5 minutes. Add raisins, cinnamon stick, anise seeds, cloves, peppercorns, and cumin seeds, and toast for another minute. Remove from heat.

recipeInstructions.name: Blend the Mole Paste
recipeInstructions.text: In a blender, combine the softened chiles, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, garlic, toasted nuts, seeds, raisins, spices, and the stale tortilla/bread. Add 1-2 cups of the reserved chile soaking liquid or chicken broth. Blend until a very smooth paste forms. This may take several minutes and require stopping to scrape down the sides. For an extra smooth sauce, strain the paste through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing firmly to extract all liquid, discarding solids. This is a crucial step for a refined sauce.

recipeInstructions.name: Simmer the Mole Sauce
recipeInstructions.text: Heat 2 tbsp of lard or vegetable oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot oil. It will splatter, so be cautious. Cook, stirring constantly, for 10-15 minutes until the sauce darkens, thickens, and loses its raw chile taste. This 'frying' step is essential for developing the mole's deep flavor. Gradually stir in 3-4 cups of the reserved chicken broth, a little at a time, until the sauce reaches your desired consistency. Add the Mexican chocolate and stir until completely melted and incorporated. Season with salt to taste, and add a teaspoon of sugar or piloncillo if needed to balance the flavors.

recipeInstructions.name: Combine Chicken and Serve
recipeInstructions.text: Add the cooked chicken pieces (whole or shredded) to the simmering mole sauce. Stir gently to coat the chicken completely. Continue to simmer for another 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the chicken to heat through. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust seasonings one last time. Serve your authentic Pollo en Mole hot.
